content.full_text:
BELL & HILTACHK
Attorneys and Counselors at Law

555 Capitol Mall
Suite 530
Sacramento CA 95814

Charles H. Bell, Jr.
Thomas W. Hiltachk                                    Telephone: (916) 442-7757
                                                      Facsimile: (916) 442-7759

May 7, 1992

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Eighth Floor
Sacramento CA 95814

Re: Request for Informal Advice

Dear Scott:

     This is a request for informal advice under Government Code
section 83114. The undersigned represents a campaign media
consultant who is seeking to obtain free time from a broadcast
station to air spot advertisements for one side of a ballot
measure. In light of the Federal Communications Commission's
("FCC") formal action to repeal the Fairness Doctrine as it
applies to ballot measure advertising, the consultant wishes to
know whether the donation of broadcast time with a value of $100
or more by a broadcast station to a committee arranged by the
consultant as the committee's agent results in a "non-monetary
contribution" to the recipient committee, if the station does not
provide equivalent donated time to the opponent.

     As you know, "contribution" is defined in Government Code
section 82015 in part as:

          "...the granting of discounts or rebates by television
          and radio stations and newspapers not extended on an
          equal basis to all candidates for the same office."

     This provision has been further interpreted by Commission advice
letters to apply to ballot measures if the discount is required
by Federal law. (Letter to Peter Hanauer, A-78-022; also, Letters
to Richard Morten, September 1988 (pending unequivocal repeal of
FCC's Fairness Doctrine for ballot measures, provision of free
time is not a contribution)).

     Although in 1987 the FCC had specifically repealed the
Fairness Doctrine as it applied to non-ballot measure committee
issue coverage, repeal as to ballot measure campaigns did not
occur until the FCC's decision in re Arkansas AFL-CIO (KARK-TV),
FCC 2d          (1992) (FCC 91-434, Memorandum Opinion and Order,
Released January 6, 1992, copy attached).

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
May 5, 1992
Page Two

     Under the Fairness Doctrine, and its corollary the Cullman
Doctrine, if one side of a ballot measure campaign could not
afford to purchase spot advertising and the broadcast station had
not met its Fairness Doctrine obligations to provide adequate
coverage of contrasting viewpoints on controversial issues of
public importance (specifically including ballot issues), the
station was obliged to provide free time to that side in ratios,
amounts, and qualities of time sufficient to correct the
imbalance.  However, none of the time provided free to California
ballot measure committees was considered reportable under these
prior FPPC interpretations.

     Because the Fairness Doctrine has now been repealed
conclusively as to ballot measures¹, the media consultant
believes that he is obliged to advise the committee he represents
that any free time obtained now in such circumstances is a
"contribution" by the broadcast station, since the station no
longer is compelled by federal law to afford such free time to a
committee.  However, if the station has provided free time on an
equal basis to both sides on a ballot measure, no "non-monetary
contribution" would be made by the station or received by the
committee.

     Because the consultant faces possible liability under
Government Code section 83116.5 if he fails to inform the
committee when he knows any reportable contribution has been made
to the committee, and as a result the committee violates the Act
by not reporting the contribution, the consultant believes he is
obliged to inform the committee if he obtains free time from a
broadcast station in such circumstances.

     I would appreciate it if you would inform me as soon as
possible about the consultant's duties with respect to this
issue.  If you have any questions about this request for informal
advice, please contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Charles H. Bell, Jr.

CHB/man
Enclosures

_____________________

     ¹ See also "Democracy by Initiative: Shaping California's
Fourth Branch of Government, Report and Recommendations of the
California Commission on Campaign Financing" (1992), at pages 218
and 219.

Before the
Federal Communications Commission
Washington, D.C. 20554

FCC 91-434
38317

In re complaint of

The Arkansas AFL-CIO and
The Committee Against Amendment 2

against

Television Station KARK-TV,
Little Rock, Arkansas

MEMORANDUM OPINION AND ORDER

Adopted: December 31, 1991          Released: January 6, 1992

By the Commission: Commissioner Barrett dissenting and issuing a statement;
                   Commissioner Duggan dissenting.


I. INTRODUCTION

     1.     This case arises from a November 2, 1990, complaint
filed by the Arkansas AFL-CIO and the Committee Against Amendment
2 ("complainants") against station KARK-TV, Little Rock,
Arkansas.  Amendment 2, which related to Arkansas' usury limits,
was an Arkansas ballot issue that was before the electorate in
the November 6, 1990, elections.  Complainants allege that the
ballot issue was a highly controversial issue of public
importance, and that KARK-TV, in light of its overall
programming, failed to give the position of those opposed to the
amendment adequate coverage as required by the fairness
doctrine.  They ask the Commission to: 1) rule that KARK-TV must
comply with the fairness doctrine; 2) find that KARK-TV has not,
in fact, complied with the fairness doctrine in its coverage of
the Amendment 2 issue; and 3) rescind its previous ruling that
the fairness doctrine is unconstitutional.

     2.     On November 14, 1990, KARK-TV responded, through
counsel, to the complaint.  KARK-TV stated that "[i]t is the
position of the licensee that the fairness doctrine is not
applicable in this situation and is unconstitutional in any
event," citing our decision in Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, 2
FCC Rcd 5043 (1987), aff'd., Syracuse Peace Council v. FCC

("Syracuse") 867 F.2d 654 (D.C. Cir. 1989), cert. denied, 110
S.Ct. 717 (1990). In addition, KARK-TV asserted that it gave
coverage to both sides of the Amendment 2 ballot issue, and did
so in a "reasonable and fair manner" in accordance with the
obligations imposed by the fairness doctrine.

3. On February 26, 1991, the staff asked KARK-TV for
additional comments on the complaint.1 The station responded by
letter dated March 14, 1991. On April 2, 1991, complainants
replied to KARK-TV's response.

4. Although the parties disagree as to whether KARK-TV's
coverage of the ballot issue was "reasonable," it is not
necessary to reach that issue here. Any requirement that
licensees provide balanced coverage of ballot issues is entirely
derived from the fairness doctrine and our decision in Syracuse
repealed the fairness doctrine. Syracuse thus governs the
outcome of this case, and the licensee therefore had no
obligation to provide any specified amount of coverage of the
Amendment 2 ballot issue.

II. BACKGROUND

5. In 1987, the Commission, faced with a fairness doctrine
complaint against Meredith Corporation, repealed the fairness
doctrine. Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, supra. After an
extensive inquiry, including comments from the public, we
concluded that our enforcement of the fairness doctrine
"contravenes the First Amendment and thereby disserves the public
interest." 2 FCC Rcd at 5057. We further concluded that the
Constitution barred us from enforcing the fairness doctrine
against the licensee.

6. The Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit upheld the
Commission on its public interest determination without reaching
the constitutional question. Syracuse, 867 F.2d at 658-59. The
court found that the court's determination that the fairness doctrine no
longer served the public interest was neither arbitrary,
capricious, nor an abuse of discretion. Id. at 669.

III. DISCUSSION

7. The fairness doctrine imposed upon broadcasters a two-
pronged obligation. Broadcast licensees were required to provide

_____________

1/ On December 28, 1990, complainants filed a Petition for
Review with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit,
asserting that the Commission's failure to render a decision on
its complaint before November 6, 1990, constituted unreasonable
delay and failure to grant timely relief. The case is pending.

2

coverage of controversial issues of public importance in their
communities and to afford a reasonable opportunity for the
presentation of contrasting viewpoints on such issues.

8.    In Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, we concluded that:

     [T]he fairness doctrine in operation disserves both the
     public's right to diverse sources of information and
     the broadcaster's interest in free expression.    Its
     chilling effect thwarts its intended purpose and it
     results in excessive and unnecessary government
     intervention into the editorial processes of broadcast
     journalists.

2 FCC Rcd at 5052.  Our conclusion that the doctrine no longer
served the public interest was based, in large part, on two core
findings in the 1985 Fairness Report, 102 FCC 2d 145 (1985): 1)
that the doctrine often operated to chill broadcaster speech on
controversial issues; and 2) that the recent increases in diversity
of available media outlets, including broadcasting
outlets, undercut the need for the doctrine. 2 FCC Rcd at
5049-53.

     9.    In Syracuse, we stated that the fairness doctrine's
infirmity of impermissibly chilling and reducing the discussion
of controversial issues was not limited to the facts of that
particular case or that particular market, "but is an infirmity
that goes to the very heart of enforcement of the fairness
doctrine as a general matter." Syracuse, 2 FCC Rcd at 5047.

     10.  Ballot issues were at the core of the fairness doctrine
because they are controversial issues of public importance in
virtually every instance.  "The existence of an issue on which
the community is asked to vote must be presumed to be a
controversial issue, absent unusual circumstances ....." King
Broadcasting Co., 23 FCC 2d 41, 43 (Broadcast Bureau 1970).
Although our decision in Syracuse did not deal with ballot
issues expressly, we based our conclusions on the 1985 Fairness
Report.  In that Report, the Commission specifically referred to
evidence of how the fairness doctrine chilled broadcasters' speech
with respect to ballot issues.  Fairness Report, 102 FCC
2d at 175-76.  Thus, our decision in Syracuse to repeal the
doctrine because of its chilling effect applies to the fairness
doctrine's application to ballot issues as well.  We have been
presented with no convincing basis to reverse our decision in
Syracuse and decline to do so.

     11.  In 1985, the Commission said that Section 315 was
subject to varying interpretations as to whether the doctrine was
codified.  Fairness Report, supra, 102 FCC 2d at 227-46.  We now
have been convinced by the analysis in TRAC v. FCC, 801 F. 2d
501 (D.C. Cir. 1986), rehearing en banc denied, 806 F. 2d 1115

                                 3

(D.C. Cir. 1986), cert. denied, 482 U.S. 919 (1987), that Section
315 is clear on its face that the fairness doctrine is not
codified.  Nothing in the legislative history persuades us
otherwise.  On this basis, therefore, we decline to award the
relief for which complainants argue.


12.  Accordingly, IT IS ORDERED, THAT the complaint filed by
the Arkansas AFL-CIO and the Committee Against Amendment 2 IS
DENIED.


FEDERAL COMMUNICATIONS COMMISSION


Donna R. Searcy
Secretary




4

December 31, 1991

DISSENTING STATEMENT OF
COMMISSIONER ANDREW C. BARRETT

Re: KARK-TV, Little Rock, AR: Fairness Doctrine Complaint

During both of my confirmation hearings, I made clear my
opinion with respect to the Fairness Doctrine. I supported the
doctrine at those times and I continue to believe in its policy
objectives. While I do not intend to push for its readoption by
this Commission, I must dissent to the determination next to apply
the Fairness Doctrine principles to ballot issues. I understand
the majority's reasoning that general elimination of the Fairness
Doctrine in the Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH case1 also
eliminated the Fairness Doctrine as it applies to ballot issues.
I do not question their logic, but do disagree with the
underlying premise that the Fairness Doctrine no longer serves
the public interest.

I think it extremely important that the voting public be
aware of all sides of controversial issues of public importance
to the community. I support the Fairness Doctrine because it
provides a mechanism to assure that more than one side of an
issue is presented. In light of today's decision, I can only be
hopeful that the majority of broadcasters will continue to
recognize that a part of "good" journalism is the coverage of
controversial issues of public importance and the presentation of
contrasting viewpoints on such issues.

1 Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, 2 FCC Rcd 5043 (1987),
aff'd, Syracuse Peace Council v. FCC, 867 F.2d 654 (D.C. Cir.
1989), cert. denied, 110 S.Ct. 717 (1990).

sections.analysis:
7. The fairness doctrine imposed upon broadcasters a two-pronged obligation. Broadcast licensees were required to provide

1/...On December 28, 1990, complainants filed a Petition for Review with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Eighth Circuit, asserting that the Commission's failure to render a decision on its complaint before November 6, 1990, constituted unreasonable delay and failure to grant timely relief. The case is pending.

coverage of controversial issues of public importance in their communities and to afford a reasonable opportunity for the presentation of contrasting viewpoints on such issues.

8. In Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, we concluded that:

[T]he fairness doctrine in operation disserves both the public's right to diverse sources of information and the broadcaster's interest in free expression. Its chilling effect thwarts its intended purpose, and it results in excessive and unnecessary government intervention into the editorial processes of broadcast journalists.

2 FCC Rcd at 5052. Our conclusion that the doctrine no longer served the public interest was based, in large part, on two core findings in the 1985 Fairness Report, 102 FCC 2d 145 (1985): 1) that the doctrine often operated to chill broadcaster speech on controversial issues; and 2) that the recent increases in the diversity of available media outlets, including broadcasting outlets, undercut the need for the doctrine. 2 FCC Rcd at 5049-53.

9. In Syracuse, we stated that the fairness doctrine's infirmity of impermissibly chilling and reducing the discussion of controversial issues was not limited to the facts of that particular case or that particular market, "but is an infirmity that goes to the very heart of enforcement of the fairness doctrine as a general matter." Syracuse, 2 FCC Rcd at 5047.

10. Ballot issues were at the core of the fairness doctrine because they are controversial issues of public importance in virtually every instance. "The existence of an issue on which the community is asked to vote must be presumed to be a controversial issue, absent unusual circumstances ...." King Broadcasting Co., 23 FCC 2d 41, 43 (Broadcast Bureau 1970). Although our decision in Syracuse did not deal with ballot issues expressly, we based our conclusions on the 1985 Fairness Report. In that Report, the Commission specifically referred to evidence of how the fairness doctrine chilled broadcasters' speech with respect to ballot issues. Fairness Report, 102 FCC 2d at 175-76. Thus, our decision in Syracuse to repeal the doctrine because of its chilling effect applies to the fairness doctrine's application to ballot issues as well. We have been presented with no convincing basis to reverse our decision in Syracuse and decline to do so.

11. In 1985, the Commission said that Section 315 was subject to varying interpretations as to whether the doctrine was codified. Fairness Report, supra, 102 FCC 2d at 227-46. We now have been convinced by the analysis in TRAC v. FCC, 801 F. 2d 501 (D.C. Cir. 1986), rehearing en banc denied, 806 F. 2d 1115

(D.C. Cir. 1986), cert. denied, 482 U.S. 919 (1987), that Section 315 is clear on its face that the fairness doctrine is not codified. Nothing in the legislative history persuades us otherwise. On this basis, therefore, we decline to award the relief for which complainants argue.

12. Accordingly, IT IS ORDERED, THAT the complaint filed by the Arkansas AFL-CIO and the Committee Against Amendment 2 IS DENIED.

FEDERAL COMMUNICATIONS COMMISSION

Donna R. Searcy
Secretary

December 31, 1991

DISSENTING STATEMENT OF
COMMISSIONER ANDREW C. BARRETT

Re: KARK-TV, Little Rock, AR: Fairness Doctrine Complaint

During both of my confirmation hearings, I made clear my opinion with respect to the Fairness Doctrine. I supported the doctrine at those times and I continue to believe in its policy objectives. While I do not intend to push for its readoption by this Commission, I must dissent to the determination not to apply the Fairness Doctrine principles to ballot issues. I understand the majority's reasoning that general elimination of the Fairness Doctrine in the Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH case1 also eliminated the Fairness Doctrine as it applies to ballot issues. I do not question their logic, but do disagree with the underlying premise that the Fairness Doctrine no longer serves the public interest.

I think it extremely important that the voting public be aware of all sides of controversial issues of public importance to the community. I support the Fairness Doctrine because it provides a mechanism to assure that more than one side of an issue is presented. In light of today's decision, I can only be hopeful that the majority of broadcasters will continue to recognize that a part of "good" journalism is the coverage of controversial issues of public importance and the presentation of contrasting viewpoints on such issues.

__________________________
1 Syracuse Peace Council v. WTVH, 2 FCC Rcd 5043 (1987), aff'd, Syracuse Peace Council v. FCC, 867 F.2d 654 (D.C. Cir. 1989), cert. denied, 110 S.Ct. 717 (1990).
